coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots with dual emission: A versatile sensing platform for rapid assay of Cr (VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Mingyan
Peng, Lulin
Yang, Maohua
Wei, Huangzhao
Zhang, Mei
Wang, Yu
coredata.dc:description: In this article, we show that the conventional boron-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BNCDs) exhibit unique fluorescent ratiometricity in the detection of Cr (VI) due to their unique dual emission system. The 3D fluorescence spectrum study reveals that besides the commonplace emission centered at (360, 465) nm in blue fluorescence region, another center which may be corresponding to N-contain conjugation domains is found at (490, 535) nm. In the presence of Cr (VI) ions, the emission centered at (360, 465) nm is remarkably quenched due to inner filter effect, whereas the emission centered at (490, 535) nm is almost unchanged. By spectrally simulating inner filter effect using different excitation interferents in the 3D fluorescence spectrum study, accompanied with the fluorescence lifetime and absorption comparison, the sensing mechanism has thoroughly been illustrated. The versatile sensing capability of BNCDs in the different real samples not only provides a new way for the rapid determination of Cr (VI) in practices, but also demonstrates the great potential of the carbon-based materials with multiple emission possess in developing ratiometric sensing platform for rapid and accurate assay of different kinds of analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual emissive fluorescence
Carbon nanodots
Inner filter effect
Cr (VI) test strip
3D fluorescence spectrum analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Cr (VI) in real samples

Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine

National Key R&D Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804132 , 82003907 , 81774202 ); Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS ( 2020185 ); National Key R&D Program of China ( 2018YFF01013904 ); Dalian Youth Science and Technology Star Project Support Program ( 2017RQ024 ); Xinglin Scholar Research Promotion Project of Chengdu University of TCM ( BSH2018011 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra, fluorescence intensity and quantum yield of BNCDs synthesized at different temperatures under the excitation of a-c) 360 nm and d-f) 490 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) TEM image of BNCDs, the down inset: high-resolution TEM of BNCDs and the up inset: the particle size distribution of BNCDs; b) FT-IR spectra of BNCDs (black curve) and their precursor APBA (red curve); high-resolution XPS spectra of c) C 1s; d) O 1s; e) N 1s and f) B 1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) UV–Vis absorption and emission spectra of the BNCDs (yellow λex = 360 nm, orange λex = 490 nm), the inset shows the image of BNCDs aqueous solution under room light and 365 nm UV lamp; b) the 3D fluorescence spectrum of BNCDs; the fluorescence spectra of BNCDs in the presence of different concentration of Cr (VI) under c) 360 nm and d) 490 nm excitation; e) the linear regression equation of BNCDs between emission intensity ratio of I 465/I 535 versus the concentration of Cr (VI) in the range from 0 to 100 μM; f) the selectivity experiment of BNCDs towards different ions in the presence/absence of Cr (VI). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The 3D fluorescence spectra and spectral overlapping of the absorption spectrum: a) BNCDs and BNCDs with b) Cr (VI), c) carmine and d) ARS. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) the image of pristine FP and BNCDs-based paper test strip (BNCDs@FP); b) the images of test strips and their brightness difference (ΔB, color level 4–50) in the presence of different concentrations of Cr (VI) under the 365 nm UV lamp; c) The linear regression equation of ΔB versus [Cr (VI)]1/3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The proposed sensing mechanism of Cr (VI) based on BNCDs.
The concentration of Cr(VI) spiked in real samples and detected by the BNCDs and ICP-OES/UV–Vis methods.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots with dual emission: A versatile sensing platform for rapid assay of Cr (VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Southwestern Chinese Medicine Resources, School of Pharmacy, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Chengdu, 611137, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Southwestern Chinese Medicine Resources
Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Southwestern Chinese Medicine Resources, School of Pharmacy, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Chengdu, 611137, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian, 6116023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, we show that the conventional boron-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BNCDs) exhibit unique fluorescent ratiometricity in the detection of Cr (VI) due to their unique dual emission system. The 3D fluorescence spectrum study reveals that besides the commonplace emission centered at (360, 465) nm in blue fluorescence region, another center which may be corresponding to N-contain conjugation domains is found at (490, 535) nm. In the presence of Cr (VI) ions, the emission centered at (360, 465) nm is remarkably quenched due to inner filter effect, whereas the emission centered at (490, 535) nm is almost unchanged. By spectrally simulating inner filter effect using different excitation interferents in the 3D fluorescence spectrum study, accompanied with the fluorescence lifetime and absorption comparison, the sensing mechanism has thoroughly been illustrated. The versatile sensing capability of BNCDs in the different real samples not only provides a new way for the rapid determination of Cr (VI) in practices, but also demonstrates the great potential of the carbon-based materials with multiple emission possess in developing ratiometric sensing platform for rapid and accurate assay of different kinds of analytes.

ce:section.ce:para: The concerns about the exposure to hexavalent chromium, a highly carcinogenic hazardous material, has not rippled the public too much until the alert was sounded by the movie “Erin Brockovich” which described the great suffering of residents in Hinkley (CA, US) due to the Cr (VI) contamination in the drinking water [1]. In the two decades later, a great amount of efforts have been put in the study of Cr (VI) contamination, including its emission pathways [2,3], geographical distribution [4], detection and removal [5,6], as well as toxicology and metabolism [7,8]. Nowadays, Cr (VI) has been identified with strong teratogenicity and carcinogenicity. It can lead to liver and kidney damage [9,10] and increase the risk of cancers including lung cancer, stomach cancer and colorectal cancer [11–13]. However, owing to its widespread application in the industrial fields such as ore smelting, leather tanning, metal electroplating, fabrics spinning and dyeing [14,15], Cr (VI) can be significantly enriched in environment through illegal waste disposal and effluent discharge from factories. The current reports have indicated that Cr (VI) contamination in natural waters and soils may be worse than as-expected in some industrial zones of China, US, EU and India [16–20]. As such, it is of vital importance to establish a network for monitoring the dynamic change of Cr (VI) concentration, which could strongly support the pollution prevention and effectively reduce the risk of excessive Cr (VI) exposure to organisms and residents.

Currently, the primary methods for Cr (VI) detection still rely on laboratorial instrumental analysis including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), atomic emission spectrometry (AES), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and ion chromatography (IC) [21–24]. Nevertheless, these methods cannot meet the requirements for rapid detection of Cr (VI) in a large scale due to their drawbacks such as expensive instruments, sophisticated operation, tedious pretreatments and time-consuming analysis. In contrast, chemical sensors based on fluorometric carbon nanodots (CDs) can avoid these issues because of their superiorities involving low cost, facile synthesis, multi-state emission, modifiable band structure, intrinsic hypotoxicity, splendid water solubility, high sensitivity, good biocompatibility and chemical stability [25–27]. As such, they are promising in the development of portable probes for Cr (VI) monitoring network.

However, most of CD-based Cr (VI) sensors are problematic in selectivity and reproducibility, because they show strong affinity for Fe3+ due to their naturally abundant phenolic hydroxyl at the surface and concentration-dependent emission. In order to solve these problems, the development of CDs with ratiometrically fluorescent sensing capability is highly desirable because the correction by calculating the emission intensity ratio of targeted and reference fluorescence can dramatically eliminate systematic error, and thus, increase accurate rate and extend the valid measurable range [28,29]. In this work, we showed that utilizing the dual emission property of the conventional boron-nitrogen co-doped CDs (BNCDs) could establish a versatile sensing platform for rapid and precise detection of Cr (VI) (Scheme 1 ). The characterizations including transmission electron microscope (TEM), fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) revealed that the as-synthesized BNCDs exhibited spherical morphology with the average diameter of 1.6 nm and were successfully doped by nitrogen and boron. The 3D fluorescence spectrum imparted that BNCDs exhibited dual photoluminescence emission centered at (λex, λem) = (360, 465) nm and (490, 535) nm, respectively. Upon the addition of Cr (VI), the emission centered at (360, 465) nm was remarkably quenched, whereas the emission center at (490, 535) nm exhibited an ignorable change. By absorption spectral comparison and fluorescence lifetime study, the quenching mechanism was ascribed to inner filter effect (IFE). Moreover, the quenching phenomenon was not affected by the co-existence of common ions. As such, a ratiometric sensor for Cr (VI) detection was developed based on BNCDs and showed an excellent linear regression in the concentration range from 0 to 100 μM of Cr (VI) by calculating fluorescent intensity ratio. In application, the real samples including lake water, textile leachate and soil extract were employed to exam the applicability of the as-developed ratiometric sensor. Moreover, a novel kind of BNCDs-based paper test strip was also developed for rapid detection of Cr (VI).

3-Aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) and K2Cr2O7 were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). CaCl2 was acquired from Chengdu Jinshan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). (NH4)2S2O8, NaH2PO4, KBr, KI were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). Other chemicals, including Na2CO3, Na2SO3, Na2SO4, CoCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Mn(NO3)2, MgCl2, Na2S, Na2S2O3, NaF, NaNO3, Zn(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, FeCl3·6H2O, EDTA disodium salt NaOH, KH2PO4, K2HPO4, HNO3, H3PO4, acetone and Cr(NO3)3 were purchased from Chengdu Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). Hg(NO3)2 were bought from Henan Xinhe Medical Device Co. Ltd (Xinyang, China). MgCl2 was purchased from Chengdu Huaxia Chemicals Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). H2SO4 was purchased from Sichuan Xilong Chemicals Co., Ltd. Deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide was obtained from Adamas-beta Chemicals Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). HEPES (N-2-hydroxyethyl-N′-2-piperazineethanesulfonic acid) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

The morphology and particle size of BNCDs were characterized by the JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan), the composition of elements and functional groups were performed by an Escalab 250xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America) and a Cary 600 Series FT-IR spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, America). The mass spectrum and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra of BNCDs of were respectively determined by a Q Exactive Orbitrap mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America) and a Bruker Avance Neo 600 MHz nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra of BNCDs were measured by a TU-1901 spectrophotometer (Persee, China), the fluorescence spectra were recorded by a F97Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Lengguang Technology, China). The fluorescent lifetime of BNCDs was tested by an FT-300 Automatic fluorescence spectrometer (PICOQuant, Germany). The concentrations of Cr (VI) spiked in the lake water and textile were measured by a PerkinElmer 8300 ICP-OES (PerkinElmer, America).

137 mg of APBA was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water, and pH of the solution was adjusted to 9.0 using 0.1 M NaOH solution. Then, a clear and homogeneous solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and reacted by oven for 6 h at different temperatures including 140, 160, 180 and 200 °C. After cooling down to room temperature, the product solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min). The supernatant was filtered utilizing a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, and the fluorescent BNCDs were obtained after dialysis against distilled water for 24 h in a dialysis kit (1000 kDa MWCO). BNCDs were dried and re-dispersed at different concentrations for further use.

The quantum yield (QY) of BNCDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as the reference. The equation of QY is:
Φ u
=
Φ s
×
F u
×
A s
×
n u 2
F s
×
A u
×
n s 2
Where Ф u and Ф s were the fluorescence quantum yields of BNCDs and standards (quinine sulfate for 360 nm, fluorescein for 490 nm), respectively. F u and F s stood for the integral of fluorescence intensities of BNCDs and standards, respectively. The absorbances of the BNCDs and quinine sulfate at the excitation wavelength were denoted as A u and A s. The refractive indexes of the solvent dissolving BNCDs and standards was marked as n u and n s, respectively.

The fluorescence titration experiment was carried out. Different concentrations of Cr (VI) including 0, 10, 20, 40, 80, 120, 160, 200, 400, 600 and 800 μM were added into BNCDs aqueous solution, respectively. The emission spectra of these mixtures were measured at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm and 490 nm, respectively.

To evaluate the selectivity of BNCDs, 200 μM of Cr2O7 2− and a series of interferent ions (200 μM) were added into BNCDs solution, and their corresponding fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at 360 nm and 490 nm excitation, respectively. 200 μM of EDTA (masking regent) was added into the solutions containing Fe3+ and Hg2+, then the emission spectra were recorded. In another interference experiment where the concentration of interferent ions were 10 times higher than Cr(VI), the concentration of Cr2O7 2− was reduced to 100 μM in order to minimize the precipitation of partial metal cation at such a high concentration. The concentration of EDTA was 2 mM in Cr3+, Al3+, Cu2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ solution, and 4 mM in Hg2+ solution.

To demonstrate the spectral characteristics of BNCDs in IFE, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of Cr (VI) (200 μM), carmine (30 μM) and ARS (100 μM) were recorded, and the 3D fluorescence spectra of BNCDs aqueous solution in the presence of these three chemicals were also obtained, respectively.
All spectra were acquired in aqueous HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4), and the concentration of the BNCDs solution was 0.06 mg/mL.

Lake water was collected from the campus of Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. An appropriate amount of the lake water was first filtered by 0.22 μm microporous membrane, and then HEPES buffer solution was added followed by adjusting pH to 7.4.

The white towels purchased from a local supermarket in Chengdu were cut into small pieces and put into 80 mL ultra-pure water. After that, 0.0296 g l-histidine, 0.4 g NaOH and 0.176 g NaH2PO4 were added into the solution. The mixture was stirred and shaken for 60 min [30], and then was filtered by 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The further treatment of the as-obtained solution was the same with that of the lake water sample.

The soil sample was collected from Shunli fastener factory (E106.74, W30.37) which involves chromium plating in Sichuan province, and was pretreated using method 3060A standardized by U.S Environmental Protection Agency with some modifications with the following steps: Dried and ground the soil sample to get the soil powder. 20.0 g NaOH and 30.0 g Na2CO3 were dissolved in water in a 1 L flask and diluted to obtain the digestion solution. After 1.25 g of soil powder was placed into a digestion vessel, 25 mL of digestion solution, 200 mg of MgCl2 and 0.25 mL of 1.0 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) were added. The sample was continuously stirred at room temperature for 10 min, then was heat to 93 °C and maintained for 1 h with continuous stirring. After cooling down to room temperature, the sample was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane . With constant stirring, pH of the filtrate was adjusted to 5.5 by adding 5.0 M nitric acid solution dropwise. The vessel was placed in an ultrasonic cleaning bath for 30 min, then pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.4. The solution was quantitatively transferred to a 50 mL flask and diluted with ultrapure water.

All the real samples were first analyzed by the BNCDs-based method. For comparison, the lake water and textile samples were further analyzed with ICP-OES and soil sample was analyzed by UV–Vis spectrophotometry method with diphenyl carbamide agent. All real samples were spiked with concentrations of Cr (VI) at the different concentration and 200 μM EDTA. The recovery in the spiked samples was calculated based on the linear regression equation obtained in the standard experiment.

The non-fluorescent cellulose filter paper was immersed in BNCDs aqueous solution (0.4 mg/mL) for 5 min and then dried in oven at 60 °C. The as-fabricated test paper was immersed in Cr (VI) aqueous solution at different concentrations including 2 μM, 20 μM, 200 μM and 2000 μM, and dried at room temperature. The images of the paper test strips were recorded with a camera under irradiation with the wavelength of 360 nm and 490 nm, respectively.

To obtain BNCDs with better optical properties, the synthesis temperature was firstly optimized. The emission spectra of BNCDs synthesized at different temperatures, including 140, 160, 180 and 200 °C, were recorded under 360 nm and 490 nm excitation, respectively. According to Fig. 1
a-b, d-e, the fluorescence intensity of product BNCDs at 465 nm and 535 nm were both enhanced obviously when the synthesis temperature increased from 140 °C to 160 °C but decreased after further increasing synthesis temperature. Besides, BNCDs synthesized at 160 °C were calculated with the largest QY under the excitation at 360 nm and 490 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 c, f. Based on these results, 160 °C was selected as the optimal temperature for BNCDs synthesis.

The morphology and particle size distribution of BNCDs were studied through TEM. The BNCDs were uniformly distributed and spherical in shape (Fig. 2
a). The particle size ranged from 0.5 to 2.4 nm, and the average size was calculated as 1.6 nm (Fig. 2 a the up inset). The HR-TEM image (Fig. 2 a the down inset) showed that the lattice spacing of BNCDs was 0.233 nm, which referred to the (100) facet of graphite [31].

The functional groups and surface elements of BNCDs were studied by the FT-IR and XPS. The IR spectra comparison of BNCDs (blue line) and APBA (red line) were shown in Fig. 2 b. The peak at 3421 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H (blue region). The peak at 1594 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the benzene ring skeleton (violet region), and the stretching vibration of B–O and C–N were found in the range of 1400 to 1280 cm−1 (green region). It was worth noting that the peaks in BNCDs were shifted comparing to those of the precursor ABPA, indicating hydrogen bonding interaction was involved in BNCDs. The XPS full spectrum of BNCDs revealed that the BNCDs consist of C, O, N and B, and the relative atomic percent of each element was 66.95%, 29.00%, 1.57% and 2.48% (Fig. S1). The deconvolution of C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2 c) gave three sub-peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.6 eV, and 288.8 eV, which could be assigned to CC, C–N/C–O and O–CO groups, respectively [32]. In the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2 d), the peaks ascribed to C–O, CO, B–O and H2O were found at 530.9 eV, 531.9 eV, 533.2 eV and 535.7 eV. As shown in Fig. 2 e, in the N 1s spectrum, peaks at 398.4 eV and 399.6 eV referred to -C-N and N–H groups, respectively. The peaks in B 1s spectrum located at 191.8 eV and 192.6 eV, indicating the existence of B–C and B–OH bonds (Fig. 2 f). These results further proved that the as-obtained BNCDs were functionalized by boronic acid and amino groups after hydrothermal synthesis.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum and steady-state fluorescence spectrum of BNCDs were recorded to investigate the optical properties of BNCDs. As displayed in Fig. 3
a, the absorption peaks centered at 260 nm and 330 nm could be attributed to the π-π∗ transition of conjugated CC, and the peak located at 500 nm could be ascribed to the n-π∗ transition of O/N-contained π-conjugation domain in BNCDs [33]. In the fluorescence spectrum, the emission maximum was observed at 465 nm under 360 nm excitation, and red-shifted to 535 nm when the excitation wavelength increased to 490 nm. Resultantly, BNCDs aqueous solution displayed a yellow color under day light and emitted bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 3 a inset). The 3D fluorescence spectrum shown in Fig. 3 b, disclosed that the shift of the emission band of BNCDs in Fig. 3a were not actually caused by their excitation-dependent fluoresence property, but originated from two different emission centers which located at λex/λem = (360, 465) nm and λex/λem = (490, 535) nm, respectively, indicating that BNCDs possessed dual photoluminescence emission and could be used for the development of ratiometric fluorescent probes.

Although the fluorescence mechanism of CDs is still an open debate, according to our previous work the multiple emission of CDs may be originated from the fluorophores formed during synthesis [34]. From this point of view, the mass spectrum and NMR study were conducted for collecting structural information of BNCDs in a molecular level. Three main species were found in mass spectrum and their molecular formula were given in Fig. S4. This result indicated that APBA firstly underwent deboration reaction to give hydroxyl aniline which further dimerized to form different N-contained aromatic species, such as C12H12N2. This assumption well explained the low atom percentage of boron in the XPS result. Interestingly, the oligomers [(C2H4O)n-4H]+H which referred to the PEG-like species, were also found in BNCDs. The 1H NMR and HSQC results further confirmed that 3-hydroxyl aniline, aniline dimers and PEG-like oligomer were formed (Fig. S5). In the previous work, we demonstrated that phenylenediamine (PDA) with different substituent site could produce different fluorophores with different emission band by dimerization during synthesis [34]. For instance, o-PDA dimerized to form phenazine-2,3-diamine which possessed an emission band centered at 565 nm. An such, it could be assumed that the dual emission of BNCDs might be originated from two different fluorophores formed by the dimerization of deborated-APBA during the hydrothermal reaction. These two fluorophores might be linked and isolated by non-conjugated PEG-like oligomers in the carbon backbone, which therefore, gave rise to two independent emission of BNCDs.

The feasibility of BNCDs-based fluorescent sensors for Cr (VI) detection was studied by Cr (VI) titration experiment. In order to achieve the best sensing ability of BNCDs, the sensing parameters including the concentration and pH effect were investigated. As shown in Figs. S2–S3, by calculating the R
i /R
0 (fluorescent intensity ratio I λ = 465/I λ = 535 before R
0 and after R
i ), the concentration of BNCDs and sensing pH were optimized as 60 μg/mL and pH = 7.4, respectively. In the titration experiment, as pictured in Fig. 3 c, the emission intensity at 465 nm gradually decreased with the increase of the Cr (VI) concentration under 360 nm excitation. However, fluorescence quenching was not observed when the BNCDs aqueous solution was excited at 490 nm (Fig. 3 d). Different from other APBA-based CDs reported previously, we utilized such unique property of BNCDs to develop a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Cr (VI) detection. By calculating the emission intensity ratio of I 465/I 535 versus the concentration of Cr (VI) in the range from 0 to 100 μM, an excellent linear regression was obtained with R2 = 0.999 (Fig. 3 f), which was better than that obtained from the single-channel calculation of fluorescence intensity change at 465 nm (R2 = 0.987, Fig. S6). The limit of detection was estimated as 0.41 μM (0.021 mg/L) according to 3 times of standard deviations of the blank solution [35], which was far lower than the maximum level of wastewater discharge (0.5 mg/L) permitted by the Chinese National Standard.

Selectivity is an important parameter to estimate the performance of a chemical sensor. The selectivity of BNCDs towards Cr (VI) and other interfering ions was investigated, including the anions of I−, F−, S2O8 2−, H2PO4 −, S2O3 2−, S2−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, SO3 2− and Br− and the cations of Mn2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Co2 +, Cu2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Fe2+ Fe3+, Hg2+ and Zn2+. It was found that only the presence of Cr (VI) led fluorescence intensity ratio (I 465/I 535) to a significant decrease while the addition of other ions had little effect on the fluorescence intensity ratio (Fig. 3 f), even when the concentration of these ions was ten times higher than Cr(VI) (Fig. S7). Although the direct addition of Fe3+ or Hg2+, two common interferent cations for CD-based sensors, gave rise to an observable influence on the detection (Fig. S8), such interference could be effectively surpassed using masking reagent EDTA. Comparing to other CD-based methods for rapid detection of Cr (VI) (Table S1), BNCDs-based method exhibited the superiority in the aspects of sensitivity and accuracy due to its ratiometric fluorometry. These results demonstrated that the BNCDs had great potential in the detection of Cr (VI) in real samples.

In order to understand the sensing mechanism of BNCDs to Cr (VI), UV–Vis spectra and 3D fluorescence spectra were performed (Fig. 4
a-d). By recording the absorption spectrum, Cr (VI) showed two intensive absorption bands in the UV region but a very weak band at 490 nm (Fig. 4 b). The strong absorption band of Cr (VI) around 360 nm overlapped with excitation band of BNCDs at λex = 360 nm, which consequentially suppressed electron transition at emission center (360, 465) nm, resulting in dramatic fluorescence quenching at 360 nm excitation due to IFE [36]. However, the emission center in the longer wavelength region (490, 535 nm) was barely affected because Cr (VI) aqueous solution had a small extinction coefficient around 490 nm.

To further validate this assumption [37], we first investigated the fluorescence lifetime at 465 nm before and after the addition of Cr (VI), as shown in Fig. S9. It could be seen that the fluorescence lifetime had almost unchanged in the presence of Cr (VI) (τ BNCDs = 53.06 ns, τ BNCDs + Cr(VI) = 53.02 ns). We next launched absorption comparison experiment for further investigation. As shown in Fig. S10, the convolution of the isolated absorption band of BNCDs and Cr2O7 2− (orange line) was almost identical with the absorption spectrum of the mixture solution of BNCDs and Cr2O7 2− obtained by experiment (blue line). These results indicated that the fluorescence quenching of BNCDs caused by Cr(VI) would undergo a dynamic process, which meant that no coordinating complex or redox product was generated after sensing. As such, given consideration to the spectral overlap between absorption band of Cr(VI) and the excitation band of BNCDs in Fig. 4 b, the sensing mechanism of the BNCDs-based ratiometric sensor towards Cr (VI) was believed based on IFE.

In order to further illustrate spectral characteristics of IFE in 3D fluorescence spectrum, two excitation interferents, carmine and alizarin red S (ARS) with different molecular extinction coefficient at 360 and 490 nm, were used for the spectral simulation experiment. Upon the addition of carmine, the emission center of BNCDs at (490, 535) nm was quenched obviously while intensity at λex/λem = 360nm/465 nm only decreased slightly, because carmine displayed a strong absorption band at 490 nm but weak absorption at 360 nm (Fig. 4 c). In the case of ARS, owing to its strong absorption both at 360 nm and 490 nm, two emission in BNCDs were dramatically quenched both in the presence of ARS (Fig. 4 d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cr (VI) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For real sample application, the BNCDs-based ratiometric sensor was used for the detection of Cr (VI) in lake water, textile leachate and soil extract samples. No Cr (VI) was intrinscially detected in the real samples, thus, the accuracy was validated through a recovery experiment by spiking a certain amount of Cr(VI) in these samples. The recovery in the Cr (VI)-spiked real samples was obtained by comparing the calculated concentration of Cr (VI) (based on the linear regression equation of I 465/I 535 versus Cr (VI) concentration) with the spiked concentration at 30, 60 and 90 μM, as shown in Table 1 . The recoveries of BNCDs-based method fluctuated between 87.2% and 111.2%, which were comparable with those in ICP-OES/UV–Vis methods. This result confirmed the feasibility and reliability of the BNCDs-based ratiometric sensor in rapid and precise detection of Cr (VI) in the real samples.

To realize the detection of Cr (VI) using BNCDs in a visualized and convenient way, we developed a paper-based test strip for the rapid screening of Cr (VI) in water. The fluorescent test strip was obtained by immersing non-fluorescent cellulose filter paper (FP) in BNCDs aqueous solution (Fig. 5
a). As displayed in Fig. 5 b, the fluorescence intensity of the test strips gradually reduced as the increase of the Cr (VI) concentration. Moreover, an excellent linear relationship was found between the brightness difference (ΔB, levels = 4–50) of strip fluorescence and cube root of the Cr (VI) concentration (Fig. 5 c). A better linearity was further obtained by calculating the brightness difference ratio (ΔB465/ΔB535, levels = 4–50) of strip fluorescence and cube root of the Cr (VI) concentration (Fig. S11). These results clearly elucidated that the as-fabricated paper strip could be used for semi-quantitative detection of Cr (VI) in the real water samples.

In this work, the fluorescent B/N-co-doped carbon nanodots with dual photoluminescence emission were utilized to develop a versatile ratiometric sensing platform for rapidly detecting Cr (VI) with high sensitivity and selectivity both in the standard and real sample experiments. The dual emission of BNCDs was assumed originating from two different fluorophores formed during synthesis. In addition, we revealed IFE-based sensing mechanism of BNCDs towards Cr (VI) by 3D fluorescence spectrum, absorption comparison and fluorescence lifetime studies. Using different excitation interferents in the 3D fluorescence spectrum study, we further illustrated the typcial spectral characteristics of IFE mechanism. The electron transition pathway in the emission centered at (360, 465) nm of BNCDs was dramatically suppressed due to the competition of photon absorption with Cr (VI), whereas the other emission centered at (490, 535) nm was barely affected, leading to a ratiometric fluorescence response. In application, a paper-based test strip fabricated using BNCDs was demonstrated capable of visualized and rapid detection of Cr (VI) in water.

Mingyan Jia: Conceptualization, synthesis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Lulin Peng: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Maohua Yang: Resources. Huangzhao Wei: Data curation. Mei Zhang: Funding acquisition. Yu Wang: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804132, 82003907, 81774202); Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS (2020185); National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFF01013904); Dalian Youth Science and Technology Star Project Support Program (2017RQ024); Xinglin Scholar Research Promotion Project of Chengdu University of TCM (BSH2018011).
